coredata.dc:title: Decorating carbon quantum dots onto VO2 nanorods to boost electron and ion transport kinetics for high–performance zinc–ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Tzu-Ho
Chen, Jheng-An
Su, Jia-He
Ting, Yuan-Hong
coredata.dc:description: Rechargeable aqueous Zn–ion batteries (RAZIBs) are regarded as attractive alternatives for large–scale energy storage. V–based cathode materials have gained great attention due to the merits of rich valence states, controllable morphology, diverse crystal structures, and controllable chemical compositions. However, the development of high–performance V–based cathode is still impeded by sluggish electron/ion transport kinetics. To address this issue, this work reveals that the electrochemical performance of the VO2 cathode can be significantly boosted by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the VO2 nanorods via a facile hydrothermal reaction. The VO2/CQDs sample is characterized to have strong interface interaction with the presence of CO bond. Compared to the CQD–free counterpart, the obtained VO2/CQDs sample exhibits higher specific surface area (18.7 vs 4.4 m2 g−1), better electrolyte wettability (53° vs 114° for contact angle), reduced charge–transfer resistance (1.8 vs 19.9 Ω), and facilitated ion diffusion coefficient (1.66 × 10−10 vs 4.56 × 10−11 cm2 s−1). Benefiting from these features, a high attainable capacity of 427 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 can be reached for VO2/CQDs, corresponding to the specific energy of 333 Wh kg−1 (based on the mass of VO2/CQDs). When the current density increases to 8 A g−1, VO2/CQDs can still deliver 309 mAh g−1, showing great promise for high–rate capability. Moreover, the VO2/CQDs cathode renders stable cycle performance with retaining 229 mAh g−1 after 2000 cycles. By contrast, the unmodified sample demonstrates moderate electrochemical performance (373 and 186 mAh g−1 at 0.2 and 8 A g−1, respectively). The results highlight the importance of the CQDs decoration strategy, which can effectively boost electron/ion transport in oxide–based cathodes for high–performance RAZIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aqueous zinc–ion battery
Vanadium oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Heterogeneous interface
Promoted electron/ion transport

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of VO2/CQDs and CQD–free samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of this work by National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan (NSTC 109–2222–E–224–001–MY3 and 112–2221–E–224–001–MY3 ) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic processes for obtaining VO2/CQDs.

(a) High–resolution TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, and (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum (365 nm) of CQDs; The inset in (c) shows the digital photos of the CQD solution under day light (left) and UV light (365 nm, right); (d) XRD pattern, (e) Raman spectrum, (f) XPS V 2p spectrum, (g) FTIR spectrum, (h) SEM image, and (i) TEM image of VO2/CQDs.

(a) Tauc's plots, (b) nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms, (c) contact angle images, (d) CV curves measured at 1.5 mV s−1, (e) EIS spectra, (f) Z′ vs. ω−1/2 plots, (g) charge/discharge curves at 0.2 A g−1, (h) rate performances, and (i) cycle performances at 1 A g−1 of VO2/CQDs and CQD–free sample.

(a) Stability test of VO2/CQDs at 4 A g−1; The inset in (a) shows a digital photo of the LED panel powered by Zn//VO2/CQDs batteries; (b) EIS spectra, (c) Z′ vs. ω−0.5 plots of VO2/CQDs measured after 100 and 1000 cycles at 4 A g−1; high–resolution TEM images of VO2/CQDs taken after (d) 100 cycles and (e) 1000 cycles.
Ex situ XPS (a) V 2p, (b) O 1s, and (c) C 1s spectra of pristine VO2/CQDs and VO2/CQDs cathodes at the discharged state (0.2 V) and the charged state (1.6 V).

head.ce:title: Decorating carbon quantum dots onto VO2 nanorods to boost electron and ion transport kinetics for high–performance zinc–ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Yunlin University of Science and Technology, 123 University Road, Section 3, Douliou, Yunlin 64002, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering
National Yunlin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Yunlin University of Science and Technology, 123 University Road, Section 3, Douliou, Yunlin 64002, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rechargeable aqueous Zn–ion batteries (RAZIBs) are regarded as attractive alternatives for large–scale energy storage. V–based cathode materials have gained great attention due to the merits of rich valence states, controllable morphology, diverse crystal structures, and controllable chemical compositions. However, the development of high–performance V–based cathode is still impeded by sluggish electron/ion transport kinetics. To address this issue, this work reveals that the electrochemical performance of the VO2 cathode can be significantly boosted by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the VO2 nanorods via a facile hydrothermal reaction. The VO2/CQDs sample is characterized to have strong interface interaction with the presence of CO bond. Compared to the CQD–free counterpart, the obtained VO2/CQDs sample exhibits higher specific surface area (18.7 vs 4.4 m2 g−1), better electrolyte wettability (53° vs 114° for contact angle), reduced charge–transfer resistance (1.8 vs 19.9 Ω), and facilitated ion diffusion coefficient (1.66 × 10−10 vs 4.56 × 10−11 cm2 s−1). Benefiting from these features, a high attainable capacity of 427 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 can be reached for VO2/CQDs, corresponding to the specific energy of 333 Wh kg−1 (based on the mass of VO2/CQDs). When the current density increases to 8 A g−1, VO2/CQDs can still deliver 309 mAh g−1, showing great promise for high–rate capability. Moreover, the VO2/CQDs cathode renders stable cycle performance with retaining 229 mAh g−1 after 2000 cycles. By contrast, the unmodified sample demonstrates moderate electrochemical performance (373 and 186 mAh g−1 at 0.2 and 8 A g−1, respectively). The results highlight the importance of the CQDs decoration strategy, which can effectively boost electron/ion transport in oxide–based cathodes for high–performance RAZIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous batteries have been revitalized as attractive energy storage systems for large–scale applications due to the merits of cost–effectiveness and intrinsic safety [1,2]. Particularly, RAZIBs have aroused increasing attention owing to the following advantages: (1) metallic Zn anode has high theoretical capacity (820 mAh g−1), low material cost (~2 USD kg−1), high abundance in the Earth's crust (~75 ppm), and meritorious compatibility with mild aqueous electrolytes [3,4]. (2) Aqueous electrolytes not only endow the batteries with intrinsic safety and high tolerance against mishandling but also provide high ionic conductivity favorable for high–rate performance [5,6]. (3) The battery components are stable under ambient environment, allowing cell assembly without the dry–room requirement. Therefore, the manufacturing cost can be significantly reduced [7,8]. Despite these features, the development of RAZIBs is restricted by a scarcity of advanced cathode materials that can deliver high capacity, superior power capability, and stable cycle performance [9–11]. In other words, obtaining high–performance cathode materials to fulfil the great potential of RAZIBs is highly desirable but still challenging.

Vanadium–based oxides have been extensively explored as cathode materials in RAZIBs by virtue of their rich valence states, controllable morphology, diverse crystal structures, and controllable chemical compositions [12–14]. These properties greatly affect the electrochemical performances of V–based cathode materials in RAZIBs [15–17]. Among various V–based candidates, tunnel–type VO2(B) is regarded as an appealing host material for RAZIBs. This material has been reported to possess suitable open structure to accommodate Zn2+ ions with a high specific capacity of 365 mAh g−1 at 0.05 A g−1 (corresponding to ~0.57 mol Zn2+ per VO2), and the migration pathways have also been found by theoretical simulations [18]. Zn2+ storage in VO2 has been revealed as reversible single–phase reaction with lattice expansion/shrinkage upon cycling, exhibiting stable performance over 5000 cycles at 3 A g−1 [19]. By scrutinizing the open structure of VO2(B), it has been disclosed that the insertion of Zn2+ is preferred along b– and c–axes due to larger open space in comparison with a–direction, as evidenced by operando analysis [20]. In addition to Zn2+, electrolyte H+ has also been identified as the charge carrier species for VO2(B) in RAZIBs [21]. In the dual–cation insertion mechanism, both Zn2+ and H+ are responsible for energy storage with their own preferred diffusion pathways. Accordingly, tunnel–structured VO2 shows enormous potential as the cathode host material for RAZIBs.

To date, several tactics have been demonstrated to improve the electrochemical performance of VO2(B) in RAZIBs. For instance, elaborately generating structural vacancies in VO2 has been reported to enlarge the tunnel size along the direction of b–axis, which can promote Zn2+ diffusion and stimulate additional intercalation sites for energy storage, leading to better electrochemical performance than the pristine sample (375 vs 280 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1; 85 % vs 65 % retention after 2000 cycles at 5 A g−1) [22]. Incorporating high–valent W6+ into VO2 lattice has been found to not only expand the lattice parameter for promoted Zn2+ diffusion but also stabilize the crystal structure via WO bond [23]. As such, WVO2 retains a higher specific capacity of ~280 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.1 A g−1 in comparison with the pure VO2 counterpart (~105 mAh g−1). Combining reduced graphene oxide (RGO) with VO2 forming freestanding electrode has been revealed to provide a porous network for efficient electron transport, showing superior rate capability [24]. Thus, when the current density increases from 0.1 to 16 A g−1, a considerable capacity retention can be achieved for RGO/VO2 (60 % vs 16 % for pure VO2). Moreover, a holey carbon–encapsulated VO2 nanobelts cathode material has been disclosed to guarantee fast electron transfer kinetics and provide abundant reaction sites, resulting in remarkable electrochemical performance (~387 and 332 mAh g−1 at 0.2 and 5 A g−1, respectively) [25]. In addition, tailored nanoarchitecture design of VOx/RGO has been confirmed to allow predominant energy storage at the heterogeneous interface between VOx and RGO [26]. The obtained VOx/RGO sample exhibits a high capacity of 443 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and outstanding rate performance with 174 mAh g−1 at 100 A g−1, thanks to the accelerated ion transport kinetics enabled by a self–driven electric field between VOx and RGO interface. In light of these advances, effectively improving electron/ion transport in VO2 holds the key to realize superior energy storage performance. In particular, integrating VO2 with carbon–based materials opens many possibilities to obtain prospective cathode materials for RAZIBs.

Among various carbon–based materials, CQDs have unique optical and electrical characteristics because of remarkable quantum confinement and edge effects [27]. Moreover, tunable surface chemistry allows to precisely regulate the physiochemical properties of CQDs [28]. Therefore, CQDs have attracted versatile applications such as electrochemical energy storage [29,30], photoluminescence [31], photocatalysis [32], and bio–sensing [33]. Integrating CQDs with V–based oxides has been reported to enhance energy storage capabilities of electroactive materials in various battery systems. For instance, surface modification of VO2 nanoarrays with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or CQDs has been revealed to improve electron/ion transport kinetics, avoid aggregation of nanostructured VO2, and mitigate the dissolution of vanadium [34,35]. Thus, the obtained composite materials exhibit promising electrochemical performance with greater capacity, higher power capability, and stable cycle performance in Li– and Na–ion batteries. For RAZIBs, incorporating CQDs into tunnel–structured V2O5 has been found to regulate the valence state of vanadium oxide, showing improved electrical conductivity for the composite cathode [36]. The V2O5/CQDs displays a remarkable capacity of 460 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and a meritorious cycling stability with retaining 175 mAh g−1 after 1500 cycles at 4 A g−1. These advances highlight the importance of the CQDs engineering strategy capable of exploiting advanced electroactive materials for energy storage.

Here, we reveal that CQDs with the averaged size of ~4 nm can be obtained by decomposing citric acid via a facile and scalable pyrolysis approach without adding other chemical reagents. The VO2/CQDs sample can be prepared by adding CQDs into the V2O5/H2C2O4 precursor for hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 1 ). As evidenced by various analyses, the decoration of CQDs endows the sample with higher surface area, better electrolyte wettability, reduced charge–transfer resistance, and facilitated ion diffusion coefficient. Therefore, VO2/CQDs exhibits an ultrahigh capacity of 427 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 (309 mAh g−1 at 8 A g−1) and superior cycle stability with retaining 229 mAh g−1 after 2000 cycles at 4 A g−1, outperforming the unmodified sample (373 and 186 mAh g−1 at 0.2 and 8 A g−1, respectively). The results signify the efficacy of the CQDs decoration strategy, which can effectively boost electron/ion transport kinetics in cathode material for high–performance RAZIBs.

10 g citric acid was put into a 250 mL glassware. It was maintained at 210 °C in an oil bath, and the precursor was kept under magnetic stirring. The reaction took 30 min after citric acid fully melt. Afterward, the glassware moved away from the oil bath, and 10 mL deionized water was added into the glassware under stirring. By removing the solid precipitate after centrifugation (8000 rpm for 30 min), the concentration of CQD solution was controlled to ~125 mg mL−1 after adding 20 mL deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of VO2/CQDs and CQD–free samples
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mmol V2O5 was added into a 125 mL oxalic acid water solution (0.08 M) under vigorous stirring. The precursor solution was transferred to an autoclave reactor for hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 8 h. After washing with deionized water and ethanol, the solid precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 8 h to obtain the CQD–free sample. The same synthetic procedures were used to prepare the VO2/CQDs sample by adding 3.32 mL CQD solution (415 mg CQDs) into the V2O5/H2C2O4 precursor solution. VO2/CQDs–1 and VO2/CQD2–2 samples were synthesized followed by the same procedures but with 1.33 and 6.64 mL CQD solution, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM–2010), UV–vis spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Lambda 850), X–ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku MiniFlex II with Cu Kα source), Raman microscopy (Renishaw, InVia confocal microscope), X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC–PHI 5000 VersaProbe III), Fourier–transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum One), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM–7610F Plus), and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (Micromeritics ASAP 2060) were carried out to characterize the prepared samples. The zeta potentials of V2O5 and CQD aqueous solutions were measured on a zeta potential analyzer (NanoPlus–3) at 25 °C.

All the electrochemical evaluations were performed in CR2032 coin–type cells consisting of the V–based cathode, Zn foil anode, glass fiber separator in 3 M ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte. The cathode was made of the prepared sample (80 %), Super P carbon (10 %), and poly(vinylidene difluoride) binder (10 %). The typical mass loading of electroactive material is ~1.3 mg cm−2. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) measurements were conducted on CH Instruments 6273D. The potential window of 0.2–1.6 V (vs. Zn2+/Zn) was investigated for CV tests, while 1.2 V with an ac potential amplitude was set to 5 mV for EIS analysis. Galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles were tested on LAND CT2001A.

In this work, citric acid was used as the carbon source to prepare CQDs via a facile and scalable pyrolysis approach without adding other chemical reagents. Fig. 2a shows the typical high–resolution TEM image of the obtained CQDs, which are well–dispersed and display particle–like morphology. The lattice spacing of 0.213 nm can be observable corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of CQD [37]. The particle size distribution further reveals that the prepared CQDs are narrowly distributed in size (in the range of 3–6 nm) with the averaged size of ~4 nm (Fig. 2b). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQD aqueous solution taken under a 365 nm lamp exhibits a strong peak at ~290 nm and a shoulder at ~330 nm (Fig. 2c). The former comes from π–π* electronic transition of aromatic CC bond, whereas the latter is attributed to n–π* transition of CO bond taking place on the surface of CQDs [38]. The CQD dispersion is a light–yellow and transparent solution under day light, while it emits blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The emitted colors of CQDs are highly associated with the size and surface functional groups [39,40]. Based on the above analyses, it can be inferred that well–dispersed CQDs were successfully prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid.

By mixing CQD aqueous solution with the V2O5/H2C2O4 precursor, the VO2/CQDs composite material was prepared via a one–step hydrothermal reaction. Notably, the electrostatic interactions between negatively charged CQDs (−7.7 mV) and positively charged V2O5 (+10.3 mV) are beneficial to form uniform decoration of CQDs onto vanadium oxide. The XRD pattern of the obtained VO2/CQDs sample is characterized as monoclinic (C2/m) VO2 (JCPDS no. 81–2392) without noticeable impurities (Fig. 2d). Further refinement reveals the crystal parameters of a = 12.060, b = 3.695, c = 6.410 Å, and β = 106.18° for VO2/CQDs (Fig. S1). Several Raman active peaks can be observed for VO2/CQDs (Fig. 2e). The signal at 141 cm−1 is related to the skeleton vibration, while the peaks centered at 193, 282, and 298 cm−1 are attributable to the bending modes of VO bond [41]. The bands located at 406, 480, 530, 689, and 993 cm−1 are assigned to VOV bending, VO3 V stretching vibration, VO2 stretching mode, VO2 V asymmetric stretching, and VO stretching vibration, respectively [42]. Interestingly, the presence of the Raman peaks at 833, 875, and 927 cm−1 indicates the co–existence of V4+ and V5+ in the compound [43]. The XPS V 2p spectrum further confirms the mixed valence states of V4+ (516.2/523.6 eV) and V5+ (517.5/524.5 eV) in the VO2/CQDs sample (Fig. 2f). The presence of V5+ in the sample reflects the incomplete reduction of precursor V2O5 by using oxalic acid as the reducing agent. Due to the absence of diffraction signals associated with V5+ compounds (Fig. 2d), the V5+ component in the VO2/CQDs sample is thought to be amorphous.

As characterized by FTIR, the prepared CQDs derived from citric acid possess rich functional groups in the particle domains (Fig. 2g). The band at 1385 cm−1 comes from COO− symmetric stretching [44], while the signals at 1634 and 1714 cm−1 are assigned to CC and CO bonds, respectively [30]. The peaks at 2962 and 3445 cm−1 are attributed to CH and OH stretching vibrations, respectively [45]. In regard to VO2/CQDs, two additional peaks emerge at 528 and 2360 cm−1. The former is associated with VOV symmetric stretching [46], confirming the presence of VO2 in the composite material. The latter corresponds to the CO bond [47]. Intriguingly, a strong signal at 2360 cm−1 is observed for VO2/CQDs but not for pure CQDs. The results suggest that VO2 and CQDs have strong interaction forming CO bond at the interface with the C atoms from CQDs and the O atoms from VO2. As characterized by SEM, the VO2/CQDs sample demonstrates nanorods morphology (Fig. 2h). The (002) lattice planes of VO2 and the (100) lattice facets of CQD can be observed in high–resolution TEM image (Fig. 2i). Evidently, the above results confirm the VO2/CQDs composite sample was successfully prepared.

VO2 is a well–known semiconductor material, and its band gap (E g) can be determined by Tauc's analysis from a UV–vis spectrum [48]. As shown in the Tauc's plots (Fig. 3a), the CQDs decorated sample demonstrates lower E g (1.09 eV) compared with the CQD–free counterpart (1.76 eV). The reduced band gap for VO2/CQDs indicates that the CQDs engineering strategy renders improved electrical conductivity, which is beneficial to achieve higher material utilization ratio (i.e., high–capacity) and better high–rate performance. Notably, specific surface area and electrolyte wettability are crucial factors influencing battery performance [49]. As characterized by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis (Fig. 3b), the specific surface area of VO2/CQDs is higher than that of the unmodified sample (18.7 vs 4.4 m2 g−1). Compared to the CQD–free sample, VO2/CQDs exhibits much better electrode/electrolyte compatibility (53° vs 114° for contact angle, Fig. 3c). Decorating CQDs onto VO2 gives higher surface area and rich oxygen–containing functional groups, thus improving electrolyte wettability of the composite material.

Overall, the obtained VO2/CQDs sample is characterized to demonstrate mixed valence state (V4+/V5+), strong interface interaction between VO2 and CQDs, narrower band gap, higher surface area, and better electrolyte uptake. These features are key factors for electroactive materials to realize enhanced energy storage performance. It is worth mentioning that mixed valence of vanadium (V4+/V5+) has been found to increase the electrical conductivity of vanadium oxide [50,51]. For the composite compounds, the incorporation of carbon–based materials has been found to regulate the electronic structure of metal oxide (or sulfide), leading to boosted electron transport kinetics [26,52]. Moreover, the strong interface interactions between metal oxides and carbon–based materials have been revealed to facilitate the electron transfer property and suppress structural degradation [53,54]. On the other hand, the presence of metal oxide/carbon heterogeneous interface has been reported to facilitate the adsorption of positively charged electrolyte Zn2+ ions, resulting in promoted ion diffusion kinetics [26]. Therefore, it is expected that the prepared VO2/CQDs sample renders efficient electron/ion transport kinetics and improved structural stability for high–performance RAZIBs.

CV analysis was undertaken to investigate the redox behaviors of the samples. Two pairs of redox peaks at ~0.78/0.48 V and ~1.18/0.87 V can be observable for both samples (Fig. 3d), corresponding to typical V4+/V3+ and V5+/V4+ redox transitions for vanadium oxides, respectively [14]. From the direct comparison, it can be clearly observed that VO2/CQDs displays higher current responses in the entire potential window. The results indicate that VO2/CQDs exhibits enhanced electrochemical performance in comparison with the CQD–free sample. EIS analysis was further conducted to reveal the electron/ion transport kinetics. As shown in Fig. 3e, the VO2/CQDs sample shows much smaller semicircle in the Nyquist plot, suggesting facile electron transfer behavior. The charge–transfer resistance (R ct) can be determined by equivalent–circuit fitting [55] (Fig. S2 and Table S1). The R ct value of VO2/CQDs is found to be 1.8 Ω, which is much smaller than that of the CQD–free sample (19.9 Ω) by a factor of ~11 times. The results substantiate that the electron transport kinetics of VO2 can be significantly improved by decorating CQDs. In addition, solid–state ion diffusivity (D) can be evaluated by the following equation [56]:
(1)
D =
R 2
T 2
2
n 4
F 4
A 2
C 2
σ w
2
where R, T, n, F, A, and C are the gas constant (8.314 J (K·mol)−1), absolute temperature (298 K), the number of electrons (take n = 2 for V5+/V3+), the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1), the geometric area of electrode (0.785 cm2), and the molar concentration of Zn2+ (0.003 mol cm−3), respectively. Followed by the determination of the Warburg coefficient (σ w) from the linear slope in Z′ vs. ω −1/2 plot (Fig. 3f), the ion diffusivity can be estimated according to Eq. (1). The calculated ion diffusivities are 1.66 × 10−10 vs 4.56 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 in the VO2/CQDs and CQD–free samples, respectively. The promoted ion diffusion coefficient in VO2/CQDs (by a factor of ~3.6 times) reveals that the incorporation of CQDs greatly facilitate ion diffusion kinetics in the VO2/CQDs cathode. As evidenced by the above results, the VO2/CQDs sample is confirmed to exhibit boosted electron/ion transport kinetics.

Galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were carried out to assess the battery performances of the samples. Both samples demonstrate two sloping regions at ~1.0 and ~0.6 V in the charge/discharge curves, reflecting the V5+/V3+ redox reactions (Fig. 3g). The results corroborate the findings from the CV analysis. The specific capacities are 427 and 373 mAh g−1 (based on the active mass of the cathode) at 0.2 A g−1 for the samples with and without CQDs, respectively. In addition to higher capacity, VO2/CQDs exhibits enhanced energy storage performance with higher specific energy (333 vs 287 Wh kg−1), benefitting from the boosted electron/ion transfer kinetics in the composite compound. Moreover, the rate capability of VO2/CQDs outperforms the unmodified sample (Fig. 3h). For VO2/CQDs, the specific capacities are determined as 381, 379, 351, and 309 mAh g−1 at 1, 2, 4, and 8 A g−1, respectively. In the charge/discharge curves measured at higher current densities, two sloping regions corresponding to V5+/V3+ redox transitions can still be observable (Fig. S3). This indicates that the increases in the polarizations are not significant for VO2/CQDs, reflecting its efficient electron/ion transport properties. By contrast, the CQD–free counterpart displays lower capacities of 244, 243, 219, and 186 mAh g−1 at the same current densities. The rate retention of VO2/CQDs is calculated as 72 % (based on the capacities obtained at 0.2 and 8 A g−1), outperforming the CQD–free sample (50 %). Accordingly, the VO2/CQDs sample shows higher attainable capacity and better rate capability, signifying its superior electrochemical performance. Notably, the specific capacity increases within the initial cycles for both samples. This capacity activation phenomenon has been reported as the irreversible oxidation of vanadium oxide to higher valence states [57,58]. Nevertheless, stable cycle performance can still be achieved for both samples over 150 cycles at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 3i). Compared to the unmodified sample, VO2/CQDs demonstrates higher reversible capacity of ~390 mAh g−1 over 150 cycles. It should be noted that different amounts of CQDs were also used to prepare composite materials (VO2/CQDs–1 and VO2/CQDs–2). Their crystal structures are also identified as monoclinic VO2(B) (Fig. S4). As characterized by SEM (Fig. S5), nanorods morphology can also be observed for both samples, although the sizes in the length and width of the nanorods are slightly different. Galvanostatic charge/discharge tests reveal that all the VO2/CQDs composites exhibit better electrochemical performance in comparison with the unmodified sample (Fig. S6). The above results distinctly manifest the importance of the CQDs decoration strategy.

The cycling stability of the VO2/CQDs cathode was evaluated at 4 A g−1. The results show that a specific capacity of 229 mAh g−1 can still be retained after 2000 cycles (Fig. 4a). A ‘Zn–patterned’ light–emitting diode (LED) panel with 17 LED lights can be powered by ten Zn//VO2/CQDs coin–type batteries. By comparing with VO2–based cathodes, the obtained VO2/CQDs is one of the most promising candidates for RAZIBs (Table S2). Moreover, the cycled VO2/CQDs cathodes are found to render efficient electron/ion transport properties, as examined by EIS analysis (Fig. 4b–c). For the charge–transfer resistance, it increases from 1.8 to 14.3 Ω after 100 cycles at 4 A g−1, and the value can be maintained as 10.0 Ω further cycling to 1000 cycles (Table S3). The ion diffusivities are 3.02 × 10−11 and 5.17 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 after 100 and 1000 cycles, respectively. Those values are slightly lower than that of the pristine cathode (1.66 × 10−10 cm2 s−1). The microstructures of the cathodes after 100 and 1000 cycles were further examined by high–resolution TEM images. The lattice planes belonging to VO2 and CQDs can still be observed in both cases (Fig. 4d–e). The results reflect that the prepared VO2/CQDs material has appealing structural stability over charge/discharge cycles, benefitting from the strong interface interaction between VO2 and CQDs. Rooted in the above results, the CQDs decoration strategy endows the tunnel–structured VO2 with facilitated electron/ion transport kinetics and appealing structural stability, leading to enhanced energy storage performance in RAZIBs.

Ex situ XPS analysis was undertaken to unravel the reaction mechanism of VO2/CQDs. The V 2p spectra reveal the changes in oxidation state within a battery cycle (Fig. 5a). The pristine electrode shows a predominant peak of V4+ along with a smaller peak of V5+. When the VO2/CQDs cathode discharges to 0.2 V, the V3+ signal (515.3 eV) emerges along with the V4+ peak [59]. The V 2p spectrum returns to V4+/V5+ pattern for the subsequent charge process. The results indicate that V5+/V3+ redox transition upon cycling is responsible for the energy storage, which is in accordance with many reported vanadium oxides in RAZIBs [59–61]. In regard to the O 1s spectra (Fig. 5b), the pristine VO2/CQDs shows several peaks located at 530.1, 530.8, 531.9, and 532.9 eV that can be assigned to CO, VO, VOH, and HOH bonds, respectively [62,63]. Upon discharging, the VOH signal becomes predominant among three peaks. This suggests that water molecules insert into the vanadium host framework along with electrolyte cations during the discharge process [63]. The O 1s spectrum detected at the charged state (1.6 V) displays similar spectral features with the pristine state. The results indicate that VO2/CQDs exhibits good electrochemical reversibility in RAZIBs. Moreover, CC (284.4 eV), CC (285 eV), CO (286 eV), and CO (287 eV) peaks can be observed in the C 1s spectra obtained at pristine, discharged, and charged states [64] (Fig. 5c). The above results confirm the presence of CO bond at the heterogeneous interface between VO2 and CQDs, signifying the strong interface interaction in the composite material. The similar spectral features further indicate that the decorated CQDs can be well retained in the VO2/CQDs cathode during the charge/discharge process. In addition, ex situ XRD analysis discloses that electrolyte H+ is also involved in the reaction chemistries. Upon discharging, the diffraction peaks originating from ZnSO4[Zn(OH)2]3·5H2O can be found (Fig. S7). The presence of this compound is attributable to the insertion of protons into the cathode material. As such, an increase in electrolyte pH induces the formation of ZnSO4[Zn(OH)2]3·5H2O precipitated on the cathode surface [65]. During the recharge process, the diffraction signals associated with ZnSO4[Zn(OH)2]3·5H2O significantly decreases, indicative of the disappearance of this compound during the H+ extraction process. Nevertheless, a weak diffraction signal can still be noticed even at the fully charged state (1.6 V), meaning the formation/dissolution of this byproduct is not completely reversible. To further identify the charge carrier species in VO2/CQDs cathode, the electrochemical performance was evaluated in 3 M ZnSO4 in ethylene glycol (EG) solvent and 1.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution. The former represents the electrolyte with pure Zn2+, while the latter reflects pure H+ condition. The results reveal that the primary charge carrier in VO2/CQDs is Zn2+, contributing to a high capacity of ca. 280 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 upon cycling (Fig. S8). The initial capacity in 1.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution is 189 mAh g−1, indicating H+ is also involved in the charge storage mechanism of VO2/CQDs in RAZIBs. Nevertheless, severe capacity decay can be noticed with retaining only 65 mAh g−1 after 30 cycles. The reason is ascribed to the significant dissolution of vanadium oxide in strong acidic electrolyte [66]. Overall, the VO2/CQDs sample is characterized to accommodate electrolyte Zn2+/H+ undergoing V5+/V3+ redox transition for energy storage in RAZIBs. The observed Zn2+/H+ mechanism is consistent with the reported vanadium oxides [21,67,68].

The current work demonstrates that integrating CQDs with vanadium oxide greatly improves electron/ion transport kinetics in the cathode and maintains its structural integrity. As a result, the VO2/CQDs composite material exhibits superior electrochemical performance with higher achievable capacity (427 vs 373 mAh g−1), higher specific energy (333 vs 287 Wh kg−1), and better rate capability (72 % vs 50 % when the current density increases from 0.2 to 8 A g−1) in comparison with the unmodified sample. More importantly, the obtained VO2/CQDs cathode has high structural stability, allowing stable cycle performance over 2000 cycles. The mechanistic studies reveal that VO2/CQDs undergoes V5+/V3+ redox transitions for energy storage in RAZIBs, and electrolyte Zn2+ and H+ ions are both involved in the reaction chemistries. This CQDs decoration strategy builds up a general approach for obtaining advanced electroactive materials for high–performance batteries.
Tzu-Ho Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition
Jheng-An Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation
Jia-He Su, Yuan-Hong Ting: Investigation, Validation

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2023.109340.
